By providing concrete examples and a step-by-step implementation process, the article illustrates how one can leverage Jira's features to perform arithmetic operations like addition and even Fibonacci sequence calculations. This exploration not only confirms Jira's computational potential but also showcases its flexibility in adapting to complex automation tasks, challenging the perception of project management tools as merely non-computational systems.

articles.title: Wayland compositor implemented as a mod in Minecraft
articles.author: thasso
articles.summary:
The article discusses a novel mod developed for Minecraft that integrates the Wayland compositor, a technology traditionally found in modern desktop environments. This innovative approach not only enhances the graphical capabilities of Minecraft but also offers players a glimpse into advanced techniques used in graphical application performance and security. By utilizing the Wayland protocol, the mod encourages a dialogue regarding potential improvements in game engine performance and user experience.

The significance of this mod lies in its potential to inspire other game developers and modders to explore similar technological integrations, leading to enriched gaming experiences. The community's involvement in shaping this mod points to the importance of collaboration in the gaming ecosystem. As this tech and gaming boundary blurs, players may find themselves benefiting from increased interactivity and efficiency, paving the way for exciting developments in future game modifications.

articles.title: Procedural Fascicle for R7RS Large Scheme
articles.author: aag
articles.summary:
The 'Procedural Fascicle for R7RS Large Scheme' is a crucial document aimed at outlining the procedural foundation of the R7RS Large Scheme. Released on May 20th, 2026, this draft serves as a vital resource for understanding the procedural features of the Scheme programming language, reflecting both its historical context and contemporary developments. The inclusion of contributions from previous editors of Scheme reports underscores a collaborative spirit and shared knowledge within the Scheme community.

This document is intended not only for academic and technical audiences but for the broader Scheme community, encouraging developers to utilize and adapt its content in their own manuals and documentation. By permitting reproduction and outlining adaptation possibilities, the writers aspire to foster growth and dissemination of knowledge about Scheme's procedural aspects, ensuring that it remains relevant and robust in an evolving programming landscape.

articles.title: .NET (OK, C#) finally gets union types
articles.author: ubernostrum
articles.summary:
The article discusses the exciting introduction of union types in .NET 11 (C# 15), a long-awaited feature that enhances type handling in C#. Union types enable developers to define variables that can represent multiple unrelated types, improving code expressiveness and explicit error handling. The author provides examples, such as Option<T> and Result<TSuccess, TError>, demonstrating how these types simplify managing different outcomes of operations.

The post emphasizes the significance of union types in modern programming practices and their prevalence in functional programming languages. It also guides the reader on implementing custom union types. Though the article is based on .NET 11 preview 4, it acknowledges that changes may arise before the official release, indicating the evolving nature of the technology and its features.

articles.title: Hershey is a textual vector font format
articles.author: Internet_Janitor
articles.summary:
The Hershey font system, established in 1967, is one of the pioneering textual vector font formats that laid the groundwork for digital typography. It encodes glyphs in a text file format where each glyph is described through a structured set of parameters which includes an ID, length, boundary positions, and a series of points that define its shape. The unique encoding system allows precise rendering and manipulation of each character, making it significant in the evolution of computer graphics and type design.

This format not only stands as an early representation of digital fonts but also illustrates the intersection of technology and visual design, embodying a creative methodology that precedes many modern font systems. The Hershey Fonts, with their vector characteristics and structured format, continue to influence both computational typography and graphics, showcasing their relevance in contemporary font rendering technologies. They serve as a historical touchstone for how text can be articulated digitally, allowing for both aesthetic appeal and functional clarity in design.

articles.title: The Architecture of BerkeleyDB
articles.author: typesanitizer
articles.summary:
The article explores the architectural design of Berkeley DB, highlighting how personal philosophies of its creators, Margo Seltzer and Keith Bostic, shaped its development. Seltzer views databases and filesystems as fundamentally alike in function, whereas Bostic emphasizes a modular approach to software engineering, advocating for simpler, component-based structures. Together, they developed Berkeley DB to provide flexible, reliable, and scalable data management that parallels traditional relational databases but in a unique library format.

Berkeley DB utilizes a modular architecture that supports fast data access and transaction recovery, aligning with the Unix philosophy of doing one thing well. The evolution of the project over two decades shows the challenges of maintaining design integrity amidst ongoing development. The initial creation of Berkeley DB was also influenced by the proprietary constraints of the time, as its developers sought alternatives in a system previously limited by licensing issues. This chapter serves as a reflection on not just the technological evolution, but also the guiding principles behind Berkeley DB's enduring design.

articles.title: Cuneiforth: A Forth for your Chifir
articles.author: neauoire
articles.summary:
The article presents a creative narrative around the discovery of an ancient disk from the late 20th century that contains the FORTH programming environment. This disk is portrayed as a vital piece of computing history, requiring optical reading techniques to access its information. The first significant portion of the disk contains a bitmap document that helps interpret the subsequent data, offering an intriguing glimpse into how early programming was stored and accessed.

The project, titled Cuneiforth, invites readers and programmers to engage with the challenge of reviving this extinct computing environment on a theoretical Chifir computer system. It emphasizes the accessibility of this task for individuals with basic programming skills and highlights the significance of preserving and understanding past technologies in the digital age. Overall, it shares both a whimsical challenge and a call to action for technologists interested in software preservation.

articles.title: remind(1) -- calendar and alarm program
articles.author: netzego
articles.summary:
Remind is an advanced calendar and alarm utility designed for UNIX and Linux systems, featuring a robust scripting language and intelligent handling of exceptions and holidays. It provides users with the flexibility of timed reminders, pop-up alarms, and output in various formats including plain-text, PDF, PostScript, and HTML. Notably, it supports both Gregorian and Hebrew calendars and operates in 12 different languages, making it accessible for diverse users. The program is released as free software under the GNU General Public License, Version 2, with the latest version being 06.02.06, available for download and installation through conventional UNIX build processes.

The creator of Remind has published a comprehensive book to facilitate user learning, along with an informative introduction video to ease new users into its functionalities. Additionally, Remind significantly contributes to users looking to manage their data privacy by not relying on external servers, aligning with the trend of de-Googling sensitive information. Users can access a public git repository for development and report any bugs found, promoting an engaged and responsive community around the software.

articles.title: z386: An Open-Source 80386 Built Around Original Microcode - Small Things Retro
articles.author: lorddimwit
articles.summary:
The article discusses z386, an open-source implementation of the 80386 CPU that utilizes the original Intel microcode, creating a functional and educational FPGA CPU. Designed to run real software, z386 successfully supports DOS operating systems and popular games, showcasing its capability to simulate historical computing experiences. As a development from the z8086 project, z386 incorporates advanced features such as a 16 KB cache and specific structures resembling the original microarchitecture, while optimizing performance through FPGA technologies.

The z386 project differs significantly from the earlier z8086, as it tackles the challenges of the more complex 80386 architecture, which includes increased instruction sets and additional functionalities like paging and protection mechanisms. The project also shares insights on the microcode disassembly process, highlighting the team efforts in understanding and reproducing the intricate workings of the original CPU design. Ultimately, the z386 serves as both an educational tool and a functional piece of retro computing hardware.

articles.title: Declarative partial updates
articles.author: nemin
articles.summary:
The article discusses the evolving needs of modern web applications, which demand dynamic content delivery beyond HTML's traditional static structure. Given that performance is critical in web environments, the Chrome team is introducing Declarative Partial Updates, which comprise new APIs to facilitate out-of-order HTML delivery. These updates allow developers to deliver content more effectively, bypassing the linear constraints of standard HTML.

These new APIs, which leverage the <template> element and processing instructions, aim to streamline how content is inserted and rendered on web pages, significantly enhancing performance and user experience. Currently available for testing in Chrome 148 and complemented by polyfills for wider compatibility, these innovations have garnered positive responses from other browser vendors, paving the way for standardization and further advancements in web development.

articles.title: What is Git made of? (2022)
articles.author: jaredkrinke
articles.summary:
The article 'What is Git made of?' provides a detailed exploration of the internals of Git, a widely-used version control system. It begins by addressing the complexity and perceived intimidation of using Git, particularly through its command line interface, yet emphasizes its clever design elements that contribute to its popularity. The article guides readers through the creation of a minimal Git repository, explaining the necessary components stored within the hidden .git directory, which are essential for Git's functioning.

Central to Git's architecture are the objects it creates and manages, which include blobs for file contents, trees for directory structures, and commits that link these together. Each object is uniquely identified using SHA hashes, with the transition from SHA-1 to SHA-256 highlighted as a significant improvement for security. The article concludes by inviting readers to build their own simplified version of Git, reinforcing practical understanding while demystifying the system’s operation through clear explanations.

articles.title: “Long-Term Support” doesn’t mean what you think
articles.author: Aks
articles.summary:
The article discusses the often-misunderstood concept of 'Long-Term Support' (LTS) in the context of various operating systems, primarily focusing on Debian, Ubuntu, and Kubuntu. It clarifies that LTS releases offer a fixed period during which security updates and maintenance are provided, but do not promise the absence of bugs, non-security issue fixes, or support for newer hardware. Each operating system defines LTS slightly differently, but the core consistency is that updates are limited to security fixes without introducing new features.

Furthermore, the article highlights how some users may feel let down when their expectations of stability and reliability are not met, due to the lack of guarantees on non-security issues. This misunderstanding stems from different interpretations of what LTS entails. Ultimately, while LTS can offer reliable security updates for a predefined duration, expectations should be managed regarding the overall performance and support of the software throughout its lifecycle.

articles.title: Pardon MIE?
articles.author: fro
articles.summary:
The article 'Pardon MIE?' explores the balance between user privacy and marketing strategies due to Apple's Mail Privacy Protection (MIE) feature. This technology is designed to obscure user data, such as email opens and locations, making it harder for marketers to track engagement and measure campaign effectiveness. As privacy becomes a priority for users, marketers are urged to adapt their strategies to maintain effectiveness without compromising user trust.

Furthermore, the article emphasizes the importance of developing new approaches to marketing within the changing digital landscape. There is a strong need for clearer guidelines on how businesses can effectively navigate the implications of MIE while promoting user privacy. User education is also deemed essential to ensure that consumers are aware of the features protecting their data, leading to more informed decisions regarding their online privacy.

articles.title: A Network Allow-List Won't Stop Exfiltration
articles.author: dergraf
articles.summary:
The article discusses the limitations of network allow-lists in preventing data exfiltration, particularly in sandbox environments that run untrusted code. While these allow-lists can effectively block unauthorized network connections, they fail to address the issue of data being transmitted through permissible channels. This blind spot can lead to significant security breaches, as demonstrated in recent malware incidents that targeted popular package registries, resulting in the theft of sensitive credentials and tokens.

To combat these vulnerabilities, the article argues for the implementation of an L7 egress proxy equipped with data-loss prevention capabilities. This solution ensures that all outgoing connections are monitored and can effectively differentiate between legitimate API calls and potential data leaks, providing a vital layer of security in the ever-evolving landscape of software supply chain threats.

articles.title: Debian SE Linux and PinTheft
articles.author: 7tehdt3cnw6kir6o
articles.summary:
The article discusses a new Linux exploit known as PinTheft and its interactions with Debian's SE Linux configuration. Initial tests reveal that the exploit fails to execute correctly when run under user_t due to access restrictions, generating multiple denied logs in the audit. However, running the exploit as unconfined_t and loading the necessary rds module allows for the successful overwriting of the page cache, demonstrating the exploit's capabilities.

The concluding observation highlights the efficacy of SE Linux in a strict configuration to thwart the exploit, emphasizing the potential need to investigate default settings regarding the rds module in Debian. The author expresses a stronger interest in the security measures offered by SE Linux rather than the management of kernel module loading, underscoring the importance of robust security configurations in preventing exploits.

articles.title: fht-compositor: A dynamic tiling Wayland compositor
articles.author: mrunix
articles.summary:
fht-compositor is a dynamic tiling Wayland compositor designed to provide an efficient window management experience inspired by traditional X11 window managers like dwm and xmonad. It features nine independent workspaces per output, allowing windows to be arranged automatically, maximizing the use of screen real estate while supporting a keyboard-centric workflow intended to enhance productivity.

The compositor not only focuses on functionality but also integrates aesthetic features to improve user experience. Developers can start working with fht-compositor through the provided documentation on the repository, which details configuration and usage. As an open-source project primarily developed in Rust, it encourages community contributions and modification under the GPL-3.0 license.
